title: Creating a 3D Layered Mandala: Part 3 - Exporting SVG & Other Cut Files
description:
Welcome to Part 3 of our engaging 3D Layered Mandala creation series. 

In the first video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NWzZCUjsv9I , we started by sketching the mandala in Photoshop, followed by a transformation into vector format using Illustrator in the second part https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DGEWCkYWqFY

Now, we're ready to make our designs versatile and widely usable. This video guides you through the process of exporting your designs in various formats, including SVG, PDF, EPS, and DXF. 
These formats ensure compatibility with popular cutting machines such as Cricut, Silhouette, Glowforge, and other laser machines.

Create seamlessly with us, and if you'd like, you can jump right into crafting by purchasing our ready-to-use mandala from our shop https://designbundles.net/pixaroma/2654600-decorative-mandala-3d-layered-svg-mandala-decor-wa

Remember to like, subscribe, and stay with us for the remaining parts of this creative journey!

Playlist with entire series here: https
transcript: hello fellow designers and crafters. I'm thrilled to present to you part three of our series  on creating a 3D layered mandala. In this video, I'll guide you through the process of exporting  the mandala we designed in part two into various file formats. These formats include SVG, DXF, AI,  EPS, and PDF ensuring compatibility with a wide range of cutting machines such as cricket,  silhouette, glow forge, and other laser machines. By covering multiple formats, I aim to empower  you to bring your mandala creations to life using the cutting machine of your choice.  So let's dive right in and explore the exporting process together.  Don't forget to hit that subscribe button and show some love by liking this video.  Now let's get crafting. As you can see, I duplicated the design and named one file decorative  mandala spread layers and the other stack layers. The stack layers file is suitable for cricket,  silhouette, and similar machines, while the spread layers file is intended for laser  machines such as glow forge. I saved the design and AI format using Illustrator 10 to ensure  compatibility with multiple software and older versions. In addition to saving the mandala design  and AI format, I proceed to save it in PDF format as well. Saving it as a PDF ensures compatibility  with a wide range of software and platforms, making it easy to share and print the design.  Furthermore, I also save a version of the design in EPS format, specifically in version 10.  EPS, encapsulated post script, format is widely supported and can be used with various  graphic design applications and printing workflows. By saving the design in both PDF and EPS formats,  I provide versatility and accessibility to users who may require different file types for their  specific needs. For the popular SVG format, I use SVG 1.1. I convert the text to outlines,  apply presentation attributes, and set the decimal places to three. The most popular format used  widely for cutting machines and web applications. For the DXF format, which is commonly used with  silhouette and some laser machines, I use the 2010 to 2012 version as the setting.  Additionally, I export the mandala design as JPG and PNG formats, ensuring high resolution  quality at 300 pixels per inch PPI. By exporting in these image formats, users can easily incorporate  the mandala into various digital projects, such as website designs, social media graphics,  or printing purposes. The high resolution setting guarantees sharp and clear details,  making the mandala visually appealing in both digital and print mediums.  For laser machines like Glowforge, I open the spread layers, version, and proceed to spread the  layers as indicated. This step is necessary because laser users typically cut the design from a  single sheet of plywood or MDF and need the elements to be spread out across the material.  Before spreading the layers, let's export each layer as a transparent PNG. To do this,  select all the layers and ensure they have a black fill and no stroke. Then go to the  Windows menu and choose Asset Export. In the Asset Export window, drag all the layers,  and you should see all five layers listed there.  In the Export setting window, where it says Scale Select Resolution and set it to 300 PPI,  choose PNG as the format. Once you are ready, click on Export and select the folder where you  want the exported files to be saved. As you can see, all the individual layers have been  successfully exported as high-quality transparent PNG files.  To spread the layers more precisely instead of dragging them, I prefer using a more exact method.  By selecting a layer and pressing Enter, you can move that layer a specific distance in either  the horizontal or vertical position. For example, if your project is 8 inches wide,  you can enter 8 for the horizontal position and 0 for the vertical position to align it exactly  at the edge. However, to avoid the laser passing over the same edge twice during cutting,  you leave a small space between the layers. You can adjust this spacing by pressing the up  and down arrow keys. Repeat these steps for all the layers until you have them nicely spread out.  After that, you can use the Artboard tool to make the Artboard larger so that it fits all the layers  accordingly.  I have saved some colors as swatches that I found on the Glowforge forum. As per my understanding,  the order in which each layer is cut is determined by its color. I prefer arranging them in that  specific order, black is cut first, blue second, and so on. Additionally, in Glowforge, if all the  layers have same colors, the software automatically groups them, making it harder for crafters to  manipulate and hide specific layers. However, you can use any colors, as long as each layer is  different. This will simplify things for certain laser machines and software.  I am creating a new folder called Color Combination Ideas. In this folder, I will include three  de-renders and color suggestions to provide customers with ideas on how they can color the three  de-layered mandal. These ideas will be further explored in the upcoming videos, specifically parts  four and five, which are related to three de-renders.
